attributes.stem: A 40-year-old man presents to his general practitioner with a six month history of restricted movements in his left hand. He has had difficulty opening his hand fully, specifically his fourth and fifth digits, with no improvement from hand and finger stretching. It has been progressively worsening, which affects certain aspects of his job as a carpenter. He has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and he is taking metformin regularly. He also has a 40 pack-year smoking history and drinks 2-3 standard drinks every day. His vital signs are within normal limits and on examination of his hand, there is skin puckering at the proximal palmar flexor crease with several nodules at the palm that are non-tender to palpation. Active and passive extension of the fourth and fifth digits on the left hand is also limited when compared to the right hand.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Palmar fibromatosis is the correct answer. Skin puckering, palmar nodules, and the presentation of limited finger extension point suggest a condition called Dupuytren’s contracture. This is a flexion contracture caused by over proliferation of fibroblasts and formation to collagen nodules and cords linked to chronic alcohol use, resulting in fibromatosis of the palmar fascia which restricts finger extension. The fourth and fifth digits are most commonly involved and skin puckering at the proximal flexor crease is usually the earliest sign. It is usually managed conservatively with physiotherapy or hand splinting, but intralesional corticosteroid injections are indicated in rapidly progressive disease. Surgery is also an option in severe disease associated with functional disability.

The patient’s presentation is not consistent with a ganglion cyst. These are benign growths that develop along tendons or joints, and can be asymptomatic or causing acute or chronic pain, depending on the location and severity. Although ganglion cysts can look similar to nodules, they typically occur on the dorsal wrist, are tender to palpation, and are not associated with any skin puckering. It is also rare that there will cause restricted flexion/extension, unlike palmar fibromatosis.

Ulnar nerve entrapment can occur at the elbow or the wrist most commonly, leading to cubital tunnel syndrome (elbow) or Guyon's canal syndrome (wrist). Although it can lead to the formation of claw hand deformity where there is inability to extend the fourth and fifth digits, these lesions are usually associated with neurological deficits such as numbness and tingling in the medial aspect of the palm and fingers, and a positive Tinel sign. It is not associated with localised features of skin puckering or palmar nodules.

Tendon sheath tumour are benign giant cell tumours of the hand that arise in tendon sheaths around the body. These tumours are more common in women, and are usually slow-growing and painless on palpation, but can cause distal numbness in large tumours compressing on the nerves of the hand. Although it can potentially form nodules in chronic disease, it most commonly affects the second and third digits near the distal interphalangeal joint (DIPJ), which is not concurrent with this patient’s presentation. 

Tenosynovitis refers to inflammation of the tendon sheath due to infectious or inflammatory factors. It usually affects a single digit and may manifest as a single painful nodule along the tendon sheath. There would also be painful finger flexion/extension, depending on the location of the inflammation (extensor or flexor tendon sheath), which limits movement.
learningPoints: Dupuytren's contracture - Fibroblastic proliferation of predominantly Type III collagen
Dupuytren's contracture typically affects the 4th and 5th digits
references: Dutta A, Jayasinghe G, Deore S, Wahed K, Bhan K, Bakti N, Singh B. Dupuytren’s contracture–current concepts. Journal of Clinical Orthopaedics and Trauma. 2020 Jul 1;11(4):590-6.
